herb_parts.name: root
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium root is not commonly used in herbal medicine due to its bitter taste and low solubility, but it does contain sesquiterpene lactones that exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Research suggests that the root may have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory effects, but more studies are needed to confirm its medicinal properties. Some traditional herbalists use the root to treat fever, colds, and digestive issues, but its effectiveness is not well-documented.

herb_parts.name: rhizome
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium rhizome is not typically considered a medicinal part of the plant as it is not commonly used in herbal medicine. However, the rhizome of some plant species in the same family as Achillea millefolium may be used medicinally.

herb_parts.name: oil
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium oil, also known as yarrow oil, is often used in aromatherapy to reduce inflammation and promote wound healing. Its antiseptic properties make it effective in treating minor cuts and scrapes, while its antimicrobial properties help to prevent infection. Additionally, yarrow oil has been traditionally used to soothe digestive issues and ease menstrual cramps due to its anti-inflammatory properties.

herb_parts.name: essential oil
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium essential oil is often used in aromatherapy to promote relaxation, reduce stress and anxiety, and improve sleep quality. It has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe skin irritations and reduce pain. The oil also has antimicrobial properties, making it effective in wound healing and preventing infections.

herb_parts.name: stem
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium stem, also known as yarrow stem, is a medicinal part that has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The stem of the plant contains a number of bioactive compounds, including sesquiterpene lactones and flavonoids, which are thought to contribute to its medicinal effects. In herbal medicine, yarrow stem is often used to treat wounds, reduce fever, and alleviate digestive issues.

herb_parts.name: leaf
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium leaf is used in herbal medicine to treat various health issues due to its anti-inflammatory properties. The leaf is also valued for its antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, making it effective in soothing skin irritations and promoting wound healing. Additionally, the leaf is used to ease menstrual cramps and other symptoms associated with PMS in women.

herb_parts.name: triterpenoids
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium triterpenoids have been found to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, contributing to their potential therapeutic benefits in treating various health conditions. These compounds have been shown to possess antimicrobial and antiviral activities, which may help in preventing infections and promoting wound healing. The triterpenoids present in Achillea millefolium may also have anti-cancer properties, inhibiting the growth of cancer cells and inducing apoptosis.

herb_parts.name: flower
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium flower is rich in flavonoids and sesquiterpenes, which have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, making it useful for treating wounds, burns, and skin irritations. The flower's bioactive compounds have also been shown to have antimicrobial and antiseptic properties, helping to prevent infection and promote healing. Additionally, the flower's flavonoids may have a role in reducing inflammation and improving cardiovascular health.

herb_parts.name: aerial part
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium aerial part is used in herbal medicine due to its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antispasmodic properties. It is traditionally employed to treat various health issues, including wounds, menstrual cramps, and digestive problems. The aerial part of the plant is also valued for its ability to soothe and calm the body, making it a popular remedy for stress and anxiety relief.

herb_parts.name: flavonoids
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium flavonoids have been identified to possess anti-inflammatory properties, which contribute to the plant's ability to soothe and calm irritated skin and tissues. These flavonoids also exhibit antioxidant activity, helping to protect cells from oxidative damage and potentially reducing the risk of chronic diseases. Furthermore, the flavonoids present in Achillea millefolium may have immunomodulatory effects, influencing the body's immune response to infections and diseases.

herb_parts.name: whole plant
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium whole plant is used in herbal medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, making it effective in treating wounds, bruises, and skin irritations. The plant's aerial parts, including leaves, stems, and flowers, are traditionally used to reduce fever and alleviate menstrual cramps. The whole plant is also used to promote digestion, ease respiratory issues, and support overall well-being.

herb_parts.name: saponins
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium saponins have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may contribute to the plant's traditional uses in wound healing and skin conditions. The saponins in Achillea millefolium have also been found to have antiviral and antifungal activities, making them a potential natural remedy for various infections. Additionally, saponins in this plant may help to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with conditions such as arthritis.

herb_parts.name: fruit
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium fruit is not commonly discussed, but the plant itself has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Achillea millefolium fruit is not widely recognized in medicinal contexts, and the plant's actual medicinal properties are more commonly associated with its leaves and flowers. However, some sources suggest that the plant's fruit may have potential anti-inflammatory properties.

herb_parts.name: alkaloids
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium alkaloids have been found to possess antimicrobial properties, helping to combat infections and promote wound healing. They also exhibit anti-inflammatory effects, making them potentially useful in treating conditions such as arthritis and other inflammatory disorders. Additionally, these alkaloids may have antioxidant and immunomodulatory activities, contributing to their overall therapeutic potential.

herb_parts.name: seed
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium seed is not commonly used in traditional medicine due to its relatively low potency and the availability of other more effective parts of the plant. However, it does contain some bioactive compounds such as flavonoids and saponins that may exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. In some herbal remedies, the seeds are used in combination with other parts of the plant to enhance their overall medicinal effects.

herb_parts.name: glycosides
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium glycosides, which include achillin and millefolin, have been found to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, contributing to the plant's traditional use in wound healing and skin conditions. These glycosides have also been shown to exhibit antioxidant activity, helping to protect cells from oxidative damage. Additionally, research suggests that achillea millefolium glycosides may have anti-cancer properties, warranting further investigation into their potential therapeutic applications.

herb_parts.name: bark
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium bark does not have a widely recognized medicinal use, however, in traditional herbal medicine, the bark of the plant is sometimes used in tinctures or decoctions, but its efficacy and safety are largely anecdotal. In some cases, the bark may be used to treat various conditions, such as fever or inflammation, but it's essential to consult with a medical professional before using any herbal remedies. More research is needed to fully understand the potential benefits and risks associated with using Achillea millefolium bark medicinally.

herb_parts.name: tannins
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium tannins have been found to possess anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe irritated skin and reduce swelling. They also exhibit antimicrobial activity, which can aid in the treatment of wounds and infections by preventing the growth of bacteria. The tannins present in Achillea millefolium may contribute to its ability to stop bleeding and promote healing.

herb_parts.name: sap
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium sap, also known as yarrow sap, has been used in traditional medicine to treat wounds and promote healing due to its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. It is believed to contain compounds such as sesquiterpene lactones and flavonoids, which contribute to its medicinal effects. The sap of Achillea millefolium has been used topically to treat skin conditions, such as eczema and acne, and to reduce inflammation and promote tissue repair.

herb_parts.name: resin
herb_parts.desc: Achillea millefolium resin is not typically mentioned in the context of the medicinal properties of this plant. However, the plant's leaves, flowers, and roots are commonly used for their medicinal benefits. The leaves, flowers, and roots of Achillea millefolium are used to make teas, tinctures, and salves for their anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antispasmodic properties.
